metadata.title:
LAB0R'S GENERAL IN


COMING COAL WAR

pages.raw_text:
John Mitchell was born at Braid
wood, Ill.,, Feb. 4, 1869, the son of a
miner and farmer. Orphaned at 4,
he was left in the care of his step
mother. His schooling was meager,
being secured only at intervals
when there was no demand for his
labor upon the farm. When only
13 years of age he entered the coal
mines at his birthplace for liveli-
hood.


At the mines he first came under
the influence of the labor movement
as then directed by the Knights of
Labor. 1t made him restless, and
he set out determined to see some
thing of the world. He visited Col
orado, New Mexico and other west
ern and southwestern states, work
ing in the coal mines. Drifting back
to the Illinois coal field in 1886, he





became a mine worker at Spring
Valley. and took an active part in
the labor movement there as head
of the Knights of Labor local Or
ganization. When % years Of age
he married Miss Katherine
O'Rourke of Spring Valley; five
children have been born to them,
of whom three are living. While
residing at Spring Valley. Mitchell
served at one time as president of
its board Of education.


He read everything within his
reach, Joined debating societies, in
dependent political reform clubs
and various social organizations
When the United mime Workers OF
America was organized in January
1890, he was among the first to be
enrolled as member In his district
He was a delegate to the subdis
trlct and district conventions; sec
retary-treasurer of the northern IL
linois subdistrict, at that time EN-
bracing all the state then organ
lied; and In 1896 chairman of the
Illinois mine workers legislative
committee. He served later as a
member of the Illinois state execu
tive board, and as a national or
ganizer.


In January, 1898. at the Colum.
bus convention, Mitchell was elect
| ed national vice president, and In
